title: Column Footing Pad Sizing 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Isolated Footing Pad Sizing by Column Load
description: Free isolated footing pad calculator. Size foundations by column load and soil bearing capacity. Instant results.
intro: Building and renovation require precise calculations: foundation dimensions, material quantities, load capacity, and code compliance. Undersized footings risk structural failure; oversized designs waste money. This calculator sizes isolated footing pads based on column load and allowable soil bearing capacity. Results current through 2026 and reviewed regularly.
keyTakeaway: Formula: **A = P / σ_adm** (Area = Load / Allowable Bearing Capacity). Direct calculation from column load and soil parameters for precise footing sizing.
useCases: Structural engineering calculations for isolated column footings

Verify preliminary sizing before detailed structural analysis

example.title: Calculation Example
steps: Column load 200 kN, soil capacity 150 kPa

explanation:
Foundation design is critical in construction. Proper sizing prevents costly overbuilding and dangerous undersizing. Building codes (IBC, ACI, AISC) establish standards for design.

## How the Calculation Works

This calculator applies the formula with your inputs, checking typical ranges and alerting when values fall outside normal parameters.

The formula is: **A = P / σ_adm**
- **A** = required footing area (m²)
- **P** = column load (kN)  
- **σ_adm** = allowable soil bearing capacity (kPa)

For a square footing, side length = √A

## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference guide. For critical decisions (building safety, code compliance), consult with a structural engineer. A soil investigation report is essential for most projects. Data current through 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: What's a typical soil bearing capacity value?
faq.a: Firm soil: 150–300 kPa. Soft soil: 50–100 kPa. Rock: 500+ kPa. Always obtain actual values from a site soil investigation report.

faq.q: How deep should an isolated footing be?
faq.a: Below the local frost line, plus a minimum of 0.5 m. Requirements vary by region—check your local building code.

faq.q: Should I round up my calculated footing size?
faq.a: Yes, round up by about 20%. Example: calculated 1.16 m becomes 1.20 m. This provides a safety margin.

faq.q: What size rebar reinforcement do I need?
faq.a: Typically 10–12 mm diameter bars in both directions. Add ties or stirrups if footing depth exceeds 400 mm.

faq.q: Is a soil investigation report necessary?
faq.a: Yes, for all meaningful projects. A professional soil study is essential to determine true bearing capacity. Typical cost: $300–1500 USD.

faq.q: Is this calculator free to use?
faq.a: Yes, all our calculators are free and require no registration.
These are reference calculations using standard, internationally validated formulas. For critical projects, have a licensed structural engineer seal your design.

faq.q: Is my data stored or sent to a server?
faq.a: No. All calculations run in your browser. We don't collect or transmit any of your data.

faq.q: What if the required footing is very large?
faq.a: Consider a larger pad, multiple columns, piles, or soil improvement. Consult a geotechnical engineer for alternatives.

faq.q: Can I use this result for my building permit?
faq.a: No. Building permits require sealed calculations from a licensed structural engineer in your jurisdiction.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
